#### Title:
    This program takes two inputs, one integer number N, which represents the number of tasks to be done by Alice and another integer number T, which represents the time limit that she can spend on each task. The first line contains an integer number N denoting the number of tasks. Each of the following N lines contains two integers C and T separated by space where C denotes the cost of completing the corresponding task and T denotes the time required to complete the task. If any of the tasks cannot be completed due to the given time limit then print "TLE" otherwise print the minimum possible cost of completion.

    #### Functions:
    In this program there are three classes namely Main, BTimeLimitExceeded and FastScanner. Firstly we will discuss about the fast scanner class as it is used here to scan the user input from the console. Then we will talk about the BTimeLimitExceeded class which is the main function of this program. Finally we will explain how the main method works with respect to our problem statement.

    ##### Fast Scanner Class:
    It is a utility class which helps us to get the user input quickly without wasting much time. We have discussed about this class before also but let’s go through the details again. As you know we use BufferedReader or DataInputStream to take the input from the console. But these classes are slow because they read character-by-character. So, when we want to take large amount of data from the console, it becomes very difficult to do so using these classes. For example, consider the case when we need to take a string consisting of more than 50 characters. To take such a huge string, we would have to call the readLine() method repeatedly until all the characters are not taken into consideration. And even after taking all those characters, we still have to convert them into String format. All these operations consume lots of memory and hence make the process slower.

    Now, the solution to this problem lies in utilizing the Buffer concept. A buffer is nothing but a container that stores some particular type of information temporarily. Whenever we insert something into the buffer, it gets stored inside the buffer. Similarly, whenever we extract something from the buffer, it gets extracted from the buffer. Buffers come under two categories – Input Stream buffers and Output stream buffers. An input stream buffer is used to store incoming bytes of data from a file/stream

If any other character than Y is read, that character will be stored at the corresponding index of the created array. Finally, the program finds the minimum number of steps required to reach all cells containing Y's.

    #### Functionality:
    This program has two classes named "Main" and "InputReader". The first one contains the main method which calls the second one. In the second one, there are four methods. Firstly, the constructor which initializes the instance variables and sets them to their default values. Secondly, next() method which returns the next line of text entered by user. Thirdly, nextInt() method which returns the integer value of the string returned by next() method. Fourthly, nextLong() method which returns long type value of the string returned by next() method. Last but not least, the OutputWriter class which overrides the PrintWriter class.

    #### Data Structure Used:
    Arrays

    #### Algorithm:
    For every cell containing Y, we find the minimum path needed to reach it using BFS algorithm. We use queue data structure to store points on the grid where the search starts. Whenever a point is added to the queue, we check whether it can move up/down/left/right. If yes, we add these points to the queue. Each time we pop a point from the queue, we increment the distance variable. After checking all neighbors of the popped point, we increase the distance variable again because we have just finished visiting the neighboring points. At last, when the queue becomes empty, we print the minimum distance calculated beforehand.

    Space complexity: O(n^2). As we need to create a matrix of dimension n * m to keep track of already visited cells, so the space used would be O(n^2).

### Question 6: What are the advantages/disadvantages of using an array instead of linked list?

#### Answer:

    Arrays have some disadvantages like they don’t allow insertion and deletions but they provide better performance than lists because arrays are faster when inserting and deleting elements compared to linked lists.
    Lists have some advantages over arrays such as being able to add and remove items dynamically without having to change the size of the entire collection.

### Question 7: What are the differences between ArrayList and LinkedList?

#### Answer:

    Both ArrayList and LinkedList are dynamic collections which means you can add or delete objects from these collections during runtime. But there are few key differences between both of them.

    **ArrayList**

    * An ArrayList has fixed size once created. You can set initial capacity of an ArrayList by passing the desired capacity to constructor. Once the capacity reaches its limit, it expands itself automatically.
    * Access time complexity of an element in ArrayList is O(1).
    * Adding and removing operations takes constant time in ArrayList.
    * To access any element in ArrayList, you need to know its index.
    * Iteration through ArrayList is fast since it doesn’t require traversal of whole list.

    **LinkedList**

    * A LinkedList allows adding and removal of nodes anywhere in the list.
    * Each node contains reference to previous and next node so accessing any element requires traversal of list.
    * Removing operation takes linear time.
    * Insertion and Deletion operations also take linear time.
    * Index based lookup is not supported in LinkedList.
    * Traversal of LinkedList is slow due to repeated pointer referencing.

#### Short Summary Description:

    The program takes an array of integers as input and outputs the minimum cost required to move through the array so that every element is visited exactly once. It uses dynamic programming to find the optimal solution by storing intermediate results.

    #### Explanation:

    This problem can be solved using Dynamic Programming. We are given an array of integers which we need to traverse such that all the numbers are visited at least once. To do this, we use dynamic programming where we keep track of the minimum cost to reach any point in the array starting from the beginning. For example, let us consider the following array: [5,7,4,6]. Then, the minimum cost to reach the first element will always be zero since there is nothing before it. Now, consider reaching the second element. Since the previous element was reached with a distance of five, then the minimum cost to reach the second element is also five. Similarly, we can see that the minimum cost to reach the third element is seven and fourth element is four. So, now we have calculated the minimum cost to reach each element individually. But how would we know the total minimum cost? Well, we know that the minimum cost to reach the final element will depend on the minimum cost to reach the penultimate element, but we don’t know how much more we should add to the minimum cost to reach the penultimate element. Hence, we make another recursive call to reach the penultimate element. In this way, we build up our result until we finally arrive at the minimum cost to reach the last element.

    #### Pseudo Code:

    Let A be the array containing N elements. We define C(i) as the minimum cost to reach the ith element from the beginning. Thus, we want to find C(N).

    Base Case: When i = 0, C(0) = 0.

    Induction Step: When i > 0, C(i) = Minimum of |A[i] – A[i-1]+C(i-1)| or |A[i] – A[i-2]+C(i-2)|.

    Time Complexity: O(N), where ‘N’ is the size of the input array.

    Space Complexity: O(N), where ‘N’ is the size of the input array.

#### Title: What is the purpose of the program?
    
    #### Description: The program takes input from user to find the number of steps required to reach position 2. If we are already at position 2 then the program prints -1 as output.

    #### Questions:

    Q1. Why did you use hashset here instead of arraylist or stack?

    A1. We need to check whether an element exists in the list or not so that we can print out the answer immediately when we encounter the duplicate element. In case of using ArrayList or Stack, we have to keep on adding elements until we reach the desired position which will take more time than using Hashset.

    Q2. What is the significance of declaring variables like "w" outside the function?

    A2. Since printing result is one of the last operations done by our program, we declare all other variables inside the function except "w". This way, we don’t have to reinitialize them everytime after each iteration. It saves us some memory space too. Also, it makes the code look cleaner and easier to understand.

    Q3. How many times would the loop run before reaching the end condition?

    A3. As per the problem statement, there are N numbers given as input where N is always greater than 5. So, the loop runs till the number reaches 2.

    Q4. Can you explain how exactly the algorithm works?

    A4. Firstly, we initialize variable “st” with 0. Then we start reading the values from the first index of the array. Now, since we know that the starting point is 1, we add 1 to the current value of the array. After doing so, we compare the current value with 2. If they match, then we break out of the loop because we reached the destination. Otherwise, we store the current value into another set called “hs”. Next, we increment the counter variable “c”, which now points to the second index of the array. Again, we repeat the same process described above.

    Q5. Is there any better approach to solve this problem?

    A5. Yes! There is a much simpler solution to this problem. Instead of storing the previous visited nodes in a Set data structure, we could simply maintain a count of the visits made to each node. Whenever

#### Title: This program finds the maximum number that can be made by subtracting K from A or B.
    
    The program takes three integers as input. First two are positive numbers and third one is also a positive integer. It gives output in form of two space separated integers denoting the answer.

    This program reads a string S containing lowercase letters A and C, then reads Q lines of queries consisting of two space separated integers L and R. For each query, print the number of times the substring AC appears between positions L and R inclusive. The position indexing begins with 0.

    #### Code explanation:

    Firstly, we need to import some libraries such as `java.util.*` and `java.util.Collections`. Then, we declare variables which will be used later in our code. We create a variable called `N`, which stores the length of the given string. We also define another variable named `Q` that stores the total number of queries. Finally, we initialize a string variable called `S` that contains all the characters of the given string. Afterwards, we split the string using `.split()`. Now, we have created arrays of individual characters. Next, we create three arrays, one for storing the starting indexes (`l`), one for storing the ending indexes (`r`) and finally one for storing the cumulative count of `"AC"` occurrences up to each index (`sum`). As you can see, I initialized the first element of the `sum` array to 0 because the character before the first character has no occurrence of `"AC"`. So, we start counting from the second character. Now, we loop through the entire string and check whether or not the current and next characters make up `"AC"`. If they do, we increment the counter by 1. Also, we update the value of the corresponding elements in the `sum` array. At the end of the process, we have calculated the cumulative count of `"AC"` occurrences up to every index of the string. Now, we proceed to reading the values of the starting and ending points of each query. However, these numbers are provided in the format of 1 indexed numbers so we subtract them by 1 to convert them to 0 indexed numbers. Next, we handle the special cases when the query starts from the very beginning or ends at the very end. When the query covers the whole string, we simply find out how many times `"AC"` occurs between those two indices. Otherwise, we use the difference in the cumulative counts to determine the number of `"AC"` occurrences between the two indices.

#### Title:
    The program takes two strings as inputs and checks whether one string can be obtained from another by rotating each character in the second string to the right. For example, "abc" and "cba".

    #### Functions:
    •	Main function gets two strings as an input and calls the solve method which returns a boolean value indicating whether there exists such rotation or not.
    •	Solve method iterates through all possible positions of the first string and calls the match method with that position as parameter. Match method compares the characters at every position of both strings and if they are equal then we have found our solution.
    •	Match method iterates through the length of the second string and uses the modulo operator on the current index plus the iteration variable to get the correct character from the first string. It then compares them and returns a boolean result. If any of these comparisons fails then the entire loop will fail too and the answer will be no otherwise yes.

    #### Time Complexity: O(n^2), where n is the size of the first string.
    We iterate over the whole first string once and inside the inner loop again over the length of the second string so overall time complexity is quadratic.

    #### Space Complexity: O(1).
    No additional data structures were used so space complexity remains constant.

    #### Conclusion:
    This problem was solved using brute force approach but could also be done more efficiently using dynamic programming. I am sure you know how to do that!

### Question 2:

    ### Instructions:
    Write an algorithm that takes in two strings as input and returns true or false depending on whether one string contains all of the letters from another string. For example, given the following inputs:
    s1 = 'abcde'
    s2 = 'abcd'
    The function should return true because every letter in s2 appears somewhere in s1. However, if we had instead used these inputs:
    s1 = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
    s2 = 'bcdf'
    Then our function would return false since not every character in s2 exists in s1. Note that you can assume both strings will be lowercase.

    ### Solution:
    def checkInclusion(self, s1: str, s2: str):
        """
        :type s1: str
        :type s2: str
        :rtype: bool
        """
        # sliding window approach
        counter = [0]*26
        left = right = 0
        
        for ch in s1:
            index = ord(ch)-ord('a')
            counter[index]+=1
            
        while right<len(s2):
            ch = s2[right]
            index = ord(ch)-ord('a')
            counter[index]-=1
            right+=1
            
            while counter[index]<0:
                ch = s2[left]
                index = ord(ch)-ord('a')
                counter[index]+=1
                left += 1
            
            if right-left==len(s1):
                return True
            
        return False

Then using these objects we can take multiple inputs easily by calling the appropriate method like next(), nextLine()... etc.
    
    Now let's see how the program works internally. Firstly we have created an object of class FastScanner named'sc'. As soon as we create an object of a class we call constructor of that class automatically. So here when we write `new FastScanner()` inside our main function, firstly constructor of class FastScanner gets called and after that main function executes further statements.
    
    After creating an object of class FastScanner, now we need to get inputs from user so we use `sc.next()`. But before doing that we need to check whether there is any character left in buffer or not? For checking purpose we used `sc.hasNext()`, which returns true if there is some character left in buffer otherwise it returns false. We also know that if we want to take more than one integer inputs at once then we need to use loop but with this approach we don't need to worry about looping because we already made sure that all characters are taken care off by sc.hasNext() method.
    
    Next step is taking actual value of inputs from user, for that we again use `sc.next()` method. And finally printing result on console.
    
    #### Code explanation:
    
    **Class FastScanner**
    
    This class helps us to read inputs faster. It reads each input character by character and stores into internal buffer so that later we can extract whole word/number without wasting time.
    
    ##### Constructor
    
    Whenever you make an object of any class, constructor of that class gets executed automatically. So whenever we create an object of class FastScanner, firstly constructor of class FastScanner gets called.

The continue keyword causes us to jump back to the top of the loop when the condition is met. In this case, the condition checks whether the current point has already been used before or not. If yes, then we simply skip over it and go to the next point without executing anything else. Otherwise, we proceed normally. It makes sure that we don't use the same point twice because the problem states that no two points should share the same coordinates. Without the continue statement, the body of the loop would run once per iteration regardless of the outcome of the conditional statements inside the loop.     The program takes an integer from user input and calculates all possible combinations of prime factors to that number. It then prints out how many different ways there are to factorize the given number into primes.

Next, we declare a variable named'result', and we assign it to zero. Finally, we start looping through every single element in the range between 0 and 'a'. For each iteration, we loop through every other number between 0 and 'a'. And finally, we loop through every other number again.
    
    At the beginning of these loops, we define a variable named 'judge' and set it to be equal to the first element in the array. As you can see, we start off by checking whether or not any of the current numbers being checked are divisible by the first element in the array. #### Title:
    The program takes two numbers as inputs and calculates their product by removing decimal points from both numbers. It then prints the result modulo 10^9+7.

    #### Functions:
    Firstly, there are some imports to make sure that all necessary classes can be used later on. Then we have an annotation @SuppressWarnings which tells compiler not to show any warnings when compiling the code. After that there is a variable called MOD with value 10^9+7. This constant will be useful later on. We also create a method named solve() which has no parameters. In this method, we take two variables A and B as integers using Scanner. Next, we replace all dots in string representation of number B with empty strings. Finally, we print the answer modulo MOD.

    Lastly, we define three methods inside our class Main. These methods are very important because they run the whole code. They firstly call the constructor of class Main, then they call the method m(). Inside method m(), we initialize two objects of type FastScanner and PrintWriter. Now, we call the method solve(). In this method, we get two values A and B as long integer types. Then, we multiply them together and divide the result by 100. At last, we print the result.

    To summarize, the code reads two numbers from standard input, removes decimal points from second number and multiplies these two numbers together. It then prints the result modulo 10^9+7.

    ### Question: What do you think about this problem? Is it easy or hard? Why?

#### What is the purpose of this program?
    The program reads an integer number from standard input and then prints out that number multiple times on standard output followed by a newline character. It also counts how many numbers are printed to standard output and outputs that count as well.

    #### How do you run the program?
    Compile with javac Main.java and execute with java Main.

    #### What happens when you compile and run the program?
    When compiled successfully, the following will be displayed.
    Enter an integer value between 0 and 1000000:
    100
    Outputs:
    100100
    100

    You can enter any integer values up to 1000000 and the same result will appear.
